text: The surgeon attempts to palpate the transverse colon to locate a lipoma, while splaying the omentum upwards. No visible intraluminal lesion is observed during this exploration.
The surgeon removes the omentum to expose the mesenteric side of the transverse colon, searching for the lipoma potentially located on the backside of the colon.
The surgeon palpates the colon, noting a slight yellow hue on the serosa, while attempting to locate the lesion seen on previous imaging studies.
The surgeon identifies a yellow area on the serosa of the colon and marks it with a suture. An intraoperative colonoscopy is then initiated.

The surgeon inserts the colonoscope through the sigmoid colon, advancing towards the proximal sigmoid and distal descending colon. A tattoo marking is visible in the descending colon, indicating the site of a previous tubular adenoma.
The surgeon advances the colonoscope towards the splenic flexure, revealing the triangular appearance of the transverse colon in the distance.
The surgeon navigates around the splenic flexure, decompressing the area to advance further. The surgeon examines a specific area, initially uncertain if it was a lipoma, but determines it is not.
The surgeon observes blood on the transverse colon, indicating trauma to the intraluminal area. The patient is positioned supine, which affects the ease of advancing the procedure.
In the distance, the surgeon identifies a lipoma on the mesenteric side of the colon and prepares to tattoo the area completely anti-mesenteric to mark the lesion for further identification.
The surgeon redocks the robotic system and introduces a new stitch near the tattoo mark on the colon, which is visible approximately a centimeter away from the original stitch site.
The surgeon stabilizes the colon and removes small fat globules from the serosal surface to clear the area. A longitudinal colotomy is then made on the colon.
The surgeon identifies the tattoo marking on the colon and cleans up the spilled black dye. The lipoma is visible adjacent to the tattooed area.
The surgeon enlarges the colotomy incision and carefully incises the mucosa without resecting it. The lipoma is then gently peeled out from its submucosal plane.
The surgeon carefully removes the lipoma from the submucosal plane of the colon, ensuring no injury to the back wall. The bottom of the lipoma is visible as the procedure concludes.
The surgeon closes the mucosal defect in the colon, avoiding resection to prevent potential injury to the back wall. The closure is intended to allow the defect to scar down and disappear.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the colon, noting that the black dye did not enhance the visibility as expected. A 3-0 micro suture is used to close the area for hemostatic purposes.

The surgeon ties the tissue to itself and prepares to close the area transversely. Observing an unusual appearance in the lower lip of the incision, the surgeon resects it to prevent bunching during closure. A three OV lock suture is used to close the incision transversely, similar to a stricture opacity technique.
The surgeon inverts the edge of the tissue to create an inner layer during the closure process.

I was pretty pleased with how this has gone so far. Again, in retrospect, I should have had the patient get a colonoscopy and tattoo the lesion prior to coming to the operating room. I did lose about half an hour to do what we did intraoperatively. But at least now that area is marked in case we ever have to go back in the future, endoscopically or surgically, and we know where that spot was.
The surgeon carefully inverts the bottom corner of the tissue during closure, ensuring that the trimmed edge remains properly aligned to prevent it from running away during the suturing process.
Dump that in as I pull tight. And then for security purposes, I'm going to run this back up.
The surgeon ties the V-lock suture to the original silk stay suture, covers the area with omentum, and prepares to undock and retrieve the specimen.
